narrative:
I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 62% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows strong commitments like planning to attend full-time and coming from an upper-middle-class family, but it also highlights some key academic hurdles compared to typical applicants. For instance, your undergraduate GPA of 2.8 sits below the average of 3.21, your LSAT score of 31 is notably lower than the usual 36.7, and your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at 1 are far below the averages around 5.5–5.7, placing you in the bottom tier academically. As a Hispanic female, you're in underrepresented groups—Hispanics make up just 3.9% of past students, and females 43.6%—but these didn't outweigh the challenges.

The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 decile, which had the biggest push toward predicting failure since it's so much lower than what's typical for bar passers. Next in influence was your LSAT score, again pulling strongly toward failure due to how far it falls from the norm. Your Year 1 decile ranked third, similarly signaling early struggles that echoed concerns for others who failed. Family income came fourth, with upper-middle status not providing the extra boost seen in some higher brackets for success. Your racial background as Hispanic was fifth, contributing toward the failure prediction in this context, followed by your undergrad GPA as the sixth strongest factor, all nudging the model toward concern.

Your gender as female was the only feature that slightly favored a passing prediction, offering a small counterbalance. Overall, these academic markers dominated because they best predict bar outcomes in our data—students with higher deciles, LSATs, and GPAs consistently pass more often. Compared to typical students who get approved, you're below average in these core areas, tipping the scales despite your full-time dedication.

To improve your chances next time, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim closer to 37 or higher, build a stronger academic record through grad courses or certifications if possible, and target schools where you can climb those deciles with dedicated study habits. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by prioritizing bar prep early, like targeted tutoring for weak subjects. Keep applying—you're full-time committed, and small wins in these areas could flip the prediction in your favor.
